title: Trauma in Pregnancy
learningObjectives: Understand the dual goals in managing the pregnant trauma patient.
Describe the physiologic changes associated with pregnancy.
Understand the pregnant trauma patient's response to hypovolemia.
Describe the injuries most commonly associated with the pregnant trauma patient.
Describe the initial assessment and management of the pregnant trauma patient.

summary.title: The Dual Goals
summary.content: Caring for the pregnant trauma patient means caring for two patients, but the best way to save the fetus is to resuscitate the mother. Prioritize the mother's airway, breathing, and circulation; what is good for the mother is good for the fetus. Any pregnant trauma patient beyond about 20 weeks gestation needs evaluation even after seemingly minor trauma.

summary.title: Physiologic Changes of Pregnancy
summary.content: Blood volume increases by roughly 40–50%, and heart rate rises by 15–20 beats/min, so a pregnant patient can lose a large volume of blood before showing signs of shock—and the fetus may be compromised while maternal vitals still look normal. Respiratory rate and oxygen demand increase, gastric emptying slows (aspiration risk), and the diaphragm is elevated by the gravid uterus.

summary.title: Supine Hypotension Syndrome
summary.content: After about 20 weeks, the gravid uterus can compress the inferior vena cava when the patient lies supine, reducing venous return and causing hypotension. Prevent or treat this by tilting the patient 15–30 degrees to the left (or manually displacing the uterus to the left), which relieves aortocaval compression and improves cardiac output—while maintaining spinal motion restriction by tilting the whole board.

summary.title: Patterns of Injury
summary.content: Common injuries include blunt abdominal trauma, and pregnancy-specific concerns such as abruptio placenta (placental separation—vaginal bleeding, abdominal pain, uterine tenderness/rigidity, and fetal distress), uterine rupture, and preterm labor. Domestic violence is an important and under-recognized cause of trauma in pregnancy—assess and document carefully.

summary.title: Assessment and Management
summary.content: Resuscitate the mother first: high-flow oxygen, support ventilation, control hemorrhage, and give fluids generously (the mother's increased volume needs replacement, and the fetus depends on maternal perfusion). Transport in the left-tilt position, monitor for vaginal bleeding and contractions, and transport to a facility capable of maternal and fetal care. Even minor mechanisms warrant evaluation because placental abruption can occur without external signs.

clinicalPearls: The best treatment for the fetus is aggressive resuscitation of the mother.
A pregnant patient can lose substantial blood before showing shock—maternal vitals can be falsely reassuring.
After ~20 weeks, tilt the patient (and the board) 15–30 degrees to the left to relieve aortocaval compression.
Give oxygen and resuscitate generously—the fetus depends entirely on maternal perfusion.
Even minor trauma warrants evaluation; placental abruption can occur with few external signs.
redFlags: Vaginal bleeding, abdominal pain, and a tender/rigid uterus suggest abruptio placenta—a fetal and maternal emergency.
Maternal hypotension or distress signals fetal compromise even when fetal signs are not directly assessed.
Supine positioning after 20 weeks can itself cause hypotension—always tilt left.
Consider and screen for domestic violence as a cause of injury in pregnancy.
commonMistakes: Transporting a late-pregnancy patient flat and supine, causing supine hypotension.
Being falsely reassured by 'normal' maternal vitals despite a significant mechanism.
Withholding oxygen or under-resuscitating the mother.
Discharging/clearing a pregnant patient after a minor mechanism without evaluation.
memoryAids: Two patients, one priority: resuscitate the MOTHER to save the fetus.
Left tilt 15–30° after 20 weeks for supine hypotension syndrome.
Abruptio placenta: bleeding + pain + tender/rigid uterus + fetal distress.
Pregnancy: +40–50% blood volume, +15–20 bpm — shock signs appear late.

decisionTrees.title: Pregnant Trauma Patient
decisionTrees.content:
1. Resuscitate the mother first: high-flow oxygen, support ABCs, control hemorrhage, generous fluids.
2. After ~20 weeks → tilt patient (and board) 15–30° left, or manually displace uterus left.
3. Assess for vaginal bleeding, abdominal pain, uterine tenderness/rigidity (abruptio placenta), contractions.
4. Recognize maternal vitals may look normal while the fetus is compromised—treat aggressively.
5. Screen/document for domestic violence.
6. Transport (even after minor mechanisms) to a facility with maternal/fetal capability.

references: International Trauma Life Support, 8th ed. Chapter 19: Trauma in Pregnancy.
Bradley, W.J. Prehospital Care of the Pregnant Trauma Patient.
